# Extracao: transcription_mp4_02-eROAS_Calculator_

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_02-eROAS_Calculator_.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.01 MB

---

It comes to tracking attribution from a Facebook ads point of view. This is one of the sheets that we look at on a daily basis. I use a concept called EROAS or eCPA to keep an eye on the contribution that Facebook is making across the kind of wider metrics of the website. So in this example, I've taken eCommerce data from a Google Analytics account associated with that store and merged it with Facebook ads data. So let me show you what that looks like here. So this data here is actually taken from Google Analytics. What I'm doing here is pulling out the sessions, so visits that have come through, by day, the number of transactions that Google Analytics has actually reported. So this is likely to be more accurate than what you're seeing in Facebook, for example. It's never going to be 100% perfect because even Google Analytics will suffer from cookie blockers and things like that, but it'll be as close as you can get generally to your sales data. So here I'm tracking my transactions in Google Analytics and also the revenue that's generated and again via Google Analytics. And I've got my average order value and conversion rate brought in. So this is all coming through Google Analytics. This is total store visits, so total website visits, total transactions, total revenue that's come through, the average order value across the whole store and the conversion rate. So analytics has brought this through. This is independent of any marketing. This doesn't care if it's Facebook or Google or Paid or Organic or whatever it is. This is just total. So on this side, we've got our Google Analytics data. Then on this side, I'm showing data from my Facebook ad account. So for that given day, for any given day, I've got my spend here from Facebook. I've got my transactions here as recorded by Facebook. And what I'm doing is to calculate an estimated return on ad spend and an estimated CPA. Let me just explain why I use this and why I keep an eye on it and why I'm looking at trends as opposed to anything else. Now, estimated return on ad spend, for many of the ad accounts that I work on, Facebook is the primary traffic driver. So we know, for example, if we're ramping up our spend on Facebook, then overall sales will go up even if Facebook are not tracking all the sales that are coming through. So what I mean by that is there are many reasons why Facebook won't see the total number of sales that come through. Number one could be cookie blockers. Number two could be errors sending that data across. But it could also be, and this is something which also does happen, Facebook only track that click for 28 days. So the default attribution set up in Facebook when you look at ads manager and you're looking at your reporting is 28 days. So if any sales come through after the kind of 28 day period when that click is made on your ad, Facebook no longer tracks it. Furthermore, a lot of you will be used to looking at your data on a day to day basis without really looking sideways at someone that clicks an ad on a given day and purchases on, you know, in two, three days time. That purchase actually gets attributed back to the day the click was made. So if that might sound a bit confusing, let me just illustrate this. If on this date here, so on the 8th of May, a click was made in an ad, yet that person bought on the 10th. Here's what happens and here's why it's maybe a slightly confusing thing to think about and also why tracking attribution is really difficult. If that click was made on the 8th, yet the purchase was made on the 5th, on the 10th, sorry on this 5th row here, Google would record the transaction on this date. Facebook would record the transaction on this date. Do you see the problem with that? So comparing like by like is really, really difficult because of that. So on this day, you know, Google Analytics has seen 735 transactions, whereas Facebook has only seen 333. At the same time, we've increased budgets here by about 2K. So the additional spend would seem to have driven some sales. Those sales would have been recorded on these days, but there's a chance that we were converting people that had already clicked. And here's an example of the journey, especially for this e-commerce store. We run a number of ads driving people for the sale and if we don't get it, we get them into an email address. So as far as Facebook has seen, for example, the click has already come through on the ad, but if we've converted them on email 2, 3, 4 days later, our analytics system will tell us that email was the source of the purchase. But the fact is email isn't a isn't out there attracting new customers in our case. It's bringing people into our funnel. So the traffic generator was Facebook. So it gets really kind of complex when you're starting to look at all the different paths and journeys. So the way we use this and the way I like to use this is I look at EROAS as a trend and a benchmark. So for example, if I know that my estimated ROAS, which looks at the return on ad spend based on total transactions generated. So here, for example, we've spent 12K on the day and we've generated 71K back in revenue, which is roughly a 600% return on ad spend. Now, it doesn't mean, especially for this purpose, that Facebook drove 100% of that revenue, but it's an indicator and I'm looking for the trends. So I look at this and say, on average, on average, our EROAS is about 400 to 500%. So that's our benchmark. If anything is severely over that or under that, then I'm going to investigate it to see what's going on. Now here, for example, I know that I've ramped up my budget and also run some promotions. Therefore, the EROAS has also gone up. It's not necessarily Facebook that's driving all of that additional revenue. A lot of it came through email, but it's an indicator and I know when that promotion has ended, it's gone back to its kind of normal. This way, I can manage my Facebook spend more effectively because if I go and look at those days, there's a chance that because, so here's the thing with this particular store, Facebook traffic, Facebook sales, direct kind of attributable, generates about 60 to 70% of total revenue. So if we look at Facebook directly, on any given day, 60% give or take, we can say this definitely came from Facebook. So where does the other 40% come from? A lot of it comes from email, from organic traffic, from direct traffic, from also from branded keywords on Google. And you look at that and say, none of those traffic channels could have created that traffic without something else contributing. So if you think about organic and paid search that's come through on the brand term, someone needs to have known about that brand. The other channels that are contributing, if we've got some other channels, they're nowhere near the level of Facebook. I ran an experiment some years ago, actually, where to prove to a business owner that Facebook's contribution was far higher than it was able to report. We actually paused ads for a week. In that time, we saw every single other traffic channel. So I'm talking about affiliate marketing, paid search, SEO, and a few others. They all took a hit. And the outcome of that, which I wouldn't recommend you do this, but literally pausing Facebook ads for a full week, it was a great experiment because it showed not only was Facebook contributing to its own sales and at the time we're getting 250, 300% rowers, the estimated return on ad spend was far higher because Facebook was contributing to all of those other channels as well. And that's why I track E-Rowers from a Facebook point of view. When Facebook is tracking at least 60, 70% of total sales, because it's a lead indicator of all of the other stuff that Facebook is contributing. Now, there are other ways that you can track the actual contribution by using lift studies and conversion studies with Facebook. But this is a quick and dirty way that we just keep an eye on things day to day to look for those trends and look at the analysis. So in your case, if RIS isn't the biggest metric, you can do the same with CPA as well. So in this case, we've looked at the total transactions that we have come in and we look at that as a factor of the cost that we've paid. So again, we're not saying here that every single transaction was generated by Facebook, but we're saying that the biggest contributor for our top of funnel traffic being Facebook can kind of tell us that if we have a look at the trends, what's performing on which day and what that CPA number is as well. So these are the two columns that are generated by merging the Facebook data with Google Analytics. And then the other thing I'm looking at is to also benchmark and trend Facebook versus GA sales numbers as well. So here, for example, we've got 198 transactions on this day and 138 via Facebook. So Google says 198, Facebook says 138. So Facebook is actually only showing 70% of total sales. And I can see from trend, 60, 70% is about normal. Here, it's kind of dropped down. It's gone back to normal and then it's dropped. It may be a cause for concern. But here, for example, I know that on these two days, we've run a promotional campaign. Email has driven a lot of it. So therefore, there's likely to be a big difference. And that for me is fine. Whereas on these days here, I may need to investigate those because I'm not entirely sure why the Facebook number has dropped below its average. So again, I'm looking for trends to identify anything that's changed against the normal. So I just want to reiterate, this isn't tracking Facebook's contribution to overall sales, but I want to benchmark and trend. Now, I've selected 14 days because generally that's a good time span for me to have a look at. You might want to look at 30 days. You can look at month-on-month, et cetera. You can set that up how you want. Now, the next question should be, well, how do you set this up yourself? So there will be a link below to take your own copy of this sheet. But getting the data in, you've got two options. The simplest one would be to download the data from Google Analytics. So you'd go into your site, you'd generate a custom report, for example. So you can do that in Google Analytics, show sessions, transactions, et cetera, download those by day, and then copy and paste those into the data sheets, which I'll show you shortly. And you can do the same for Facebook. But as you can see here on the right-hand side, I like to use supermetrics for this. So this is another cool reason and way to use supermetrics, which is you can merge your data sources. So here, I've already done the selection just to show you quickly how to do this. So first of all, you go into your data sources and select Google Analytics. At that point, it will ask you to log into your Google Analytics account. You'll go away, you'll log in and come back. Then it will pull out all of the different views and sources from your Analytics setup. So on this one, I've selected one here. Then you select your date. So I've selected the last two weeks. Then you select your metrics. Now here, I'm pulling out sessions, transactions, transaction revenue, which I haven't put. And then also the average order values. So you can just see I'm adding that here. So I'm pulling out these four metrics. Now I've calculated conversion rate myself here. You can pull out conversion rate here as well. So I think that would be e-commerce conversion rate. So you can kind of do that there as well. So there, that's the data that I'm pulling out. And then what I would do is I'm pulling out the date and also the view name. And then I'm not putting any segments, no filters. And I will just, I think this defaults is on. So I put that as off. Now if I show you what that looks like in the data tab, I've highlighted in yellow where you need to drop the data because there are calculations outside of that, which would otherwise not work properly. So once you've run this, in fact, once you've set the query up, you would set your cursor here to tell supermetrics to drop the data here. And then you'd get the data to table and it will drop your data into these columns. So it drops it by your split. So data and view name, which will go here. And then into these columns. And then the rest of it's done. Now one thing to bear in mind is whatever the name of your view here is, make sure that you type it in here. So that's the way that the lookups will do its match and the spreadsheet will do its searching to find all the data. So just make sure that matches up. The second tab in terms of pulling the Facebook ads data is the exact same raw data that we had set up previously for the previous reports. But you can have a look into the raw data to see what columns and data needs to be pulled in from there. But it's fairly basic. So as long as you've gone through the KPI setup and dashboarding in the earlier parts of the training, then you'll know how to set up the Facebook data. And then it will pull it through automatically. So it's looking for the raw data tab and then we'll know what to look for and pull the data through. So that's pretty easy to do. With Supermetrics, I automate this. So this updates every hour or so. So every time I go into this for any of my ad accounts, and I will have multiple versions of these loading up in one spreadsheet, then I can immediately see what's going on at a glance. I use my conditional formatting, which I find easier to visually see what, you know, which days are better, which days are worse. And then that way I can just have a quick glance to see what my attribution is looking like and whether there is anything I need to dive into further to do some analysis, etc. So in the previous part of this training, we've talked about different ways you can view attribution. And you can go really deep into this. And honestly, you can get lost as well. But this sheet helps at high level just to keep an eye on everything that's going on.
